raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "A new bill moving through the California Legislature will stop dental insurance companies from scamming families by collecting premiums while making it nearly impossible for families to use their benefits.",
    "Assembly Bill 371 requires dental insurers to provide access to an in-network dentist near a patient’s home or workplace.",
    "The bill bans dental insurers from forcing families to pay out-of-pocket for care that their policy is supposed to cover.",
    "Dental insurance providers intentionally make it difficult to use their insurance by limiting the number of dentists available in-network.",
    "Families in California are unable to get appointments with overcrowded local dentists or must drive over an hour to find an in-network dentist.",
    "Dental insurance often requires families to pay more than 42% out-of-pocket, even when they have coverage.",
    "Nearly 2 million Americans turn to emergency rooms for untreated dental pain each year.",
    "California ranks 47th in the nation for children’s dental health, with low-income communities suffering the worst outcomes.",
    "Despite having one of the highest numbers of licensed dentists in the country, patients still struggle to get an appointment due to limited insurance networks.",
    "Dental insurers continue to limit what they pay for care while generating record profits.",
    "Many employer-sponsored dental plans still cap annual benefits at $1,000–$2,000, a limit that hasn’t changed in 50 years.",
    "AB 371 requires insurers to provide in-network dentists within 15 miles and specialists within 30 miles.",
    "AB 371 mandates transparency in provider networks, requiring insurers to create real, accessible networks.",
    "AB 371 prevents insurers from forcing patients to pay out-of-pocket for covered care, ensuring dentists get paid directly by insurance.",
    "Insurers argue that expanding networks could raise costs, while advocates claim the real issue is corporate greed.",
    "Other states, including Colorado, Oregon, and Maryland, already enforce stronger dental network requirements."
  ]
}
```
